items.question: Define TP, FP, TN, and FN for binary classification with positive class = 1.
items.answer: TP: actual 1, predicted 1. FP: actual 0, predicted 1 (false alarm). TN: actual 0, predicted 0. FN: actual 1, predicted 0 (miss). The four counts partition the evaluation set and sum to N.

items.question: In sklearn's confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred), what do rows and columns represent?
items.answer: Rows are actual (true) labels; columns are predicted labels. For binary labels [0, 1], cm[0,0]=TN, cm[0,1]=FP, cm[1,0]=FN, cm[1,1]=TP.

items.question: How do you compute TPR and FPR from a confusion matrix?
items.answer: TPR (recall/sensitivity) = TP / (TP + FN) — fraction of actual positives caught. FPR = FP / (FP + TN) — fraction of actual negatives falsely flagged as positive.

items.question: Why can accuracy be misleading when classes are imbalanced?
items.answer: Accuracy = (TP+TN)/N weights all examples equally. A majority-negative model can score high accuracy while FN captures most rare positives — the matrix reveals low TP and high FN on the minority row.

items.question: How does a confusion matrix relate to ROC curves?
items.answer: Each ROC point comes from one threshold-induced confusion matrix — compute TP, FP, TN, FN at that cutoff, then TPR=TP/(TP+FN) and FPR=FP/(FP+TN). ROC sweeps thresholds; one matrix fixes a single cutoff.
